file_name: Hussain et al. - 2023 - Transfer learning-based quantized deep learning models for nail melanoma classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning and transfer learning to classify nail melanoma. Specifically, it trained and evaluated seven CNN-based architectures (VGG19, ResNet101, ResNet152V2, Xception, InceptionV3, MobileNet, and MobileNetv2) on a custom dataset (Nailmelanoma) for the task of nail melanoma classification and localization.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the collection of a new dataset (Nail-Melanoma-350), the training and evaluation of seven CNN-based deep learning architectures using transfer learning, and the application of quantization techniques. It reports specific performance metrics (e.g., >95% accuracy, 96% for ResNet152V2) and validates the models on a custom dataset, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Transfer learning-based quantized deep learning models for nail melanoma classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early nail melanoma detection by introducing a new custom dataset, Nail-Melanoma-350, comprising dermoscopic images of healthy and malignant nails. The authors trained and evaluated seven CNN-based transfer learning architectures, including VGG19, ResNet101, ResNet152V2, Xception, InceptionV3, MobileNet, and MobileNetv2, to classify skin lesions. The models were validated using key performance metrics such as accuracy, recall, specificity, precision, and F1-score, with data augmentation employed to enhance generalization. The results demonstrated that the proposed workflow achieved greater than 95% accuracy, with ResNet152V2 performing particularly well. Furthermore, the study demonstrated that post-quantization techniques could effectively reduce the memory footprint of these models, making them suitable for deployment on memory-constrained mobile and edge devices. This approach offers a robust tool for timely and accurate diagnosis of nail melanoma in clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the classification task. The study introduces a custom dataset 'Nail-Melanoma-350' containing images of nail melanoma (123 training, 52 testing) and healthy nails. The paper also references 'acral melanoma' in the context of related literature (Abbas et al. [31]) and mentions evaluating performance across melanoma subtypes like superficial spreading, lentigo malignant melanoma, and nodular melanoma in cited works, but the experimental focus is on nail melanoma.

The negative class used for binary classification against nail melanoma. The dataset includes 123 healthy nail images for training and 52 for testing. The abstract and introduction refer to distinguishing malignant tumors from 'normal cells' or 'healthy nails'.

all_task_types.target: Nail melanoma vs. healthy nail (binary classification)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'nail melanoma classification' and 'classification of skin lesions for melanoma detection'. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (malignant melanoma vs. normal/healthy nails) using CNN architectures (VGG19, ResNet101, etc.) on the Nail-Melanoma-350 dataset. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, recall, specificity, precision, and F1-score, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Nail-Melanoma-350
datasets_used.description: A custom, indigenous dataset of dermoscopic images collected from Dow International Medical College in Karachi, Pakistan, annotated by experts. It was created to address the lack of public nail melanoma datasets and includes images of healthy nails and nail melanoma lesions.

use.training: The original 245 images (123 healthy, 123 melanoma) were used for training. After data augmentation (rotation, flips, shifts), the training set was expanded to 1600 images (700 healthy, 700 melanoma) to improve model generalization and reduce overfitting.
use.testing: The original 105 images (52 healthy, 52 melanoma) were used for testing. After augmentation, the testing set was expanded to 400 images (300 healthy, 300 melanoma) for evaluation.
use.other: The dataset was also used to demonstrate the feasibility of deploying quantized deep learning models on memory-constrained mobile/edge devices.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, recall, specificity, precision, and F1-score.

Nail-Melanoma-350.notes: Results for models after compressing with quantization. Quantization reduced memory footprint while maintaining performance suitable for edge devices.

architecture_used.main_model: Seven CNN-based architectures: VGG19, ResNet101, ResNet152V2, Xception, InceptionV3, MobileNet, MobileNetv2, and UNet.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights from ImageNet were used for the CNN models. UNet was also tested.

architecture_details.technique: Transfer learning with post-quantization (TFLite compression) for memory-constrained edge devices.

training_details.hardware: HP Z600 with 24 Core Xeon processors, 32GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 Super Graphics Card
training_details.loss_function: Categorical cross-entropy (implied by formula J_CNN in context)

classification_or_output_head.task: Binary classification (Nail melanoma vs. Healthy nail)
classification_or_output_head.output: Quantized model output for edge device deployment

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of all samples in the combined dataset
Resizing images to 256 x 256 pixels to match the input of pre-trained models
Data augmentation using the Albumentations library, including random rotation (45 degrees), horizontal and vertical flips, height shift, and width shift

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Original training set of 245 images (123 healthy, 123 melanoma) was expanded to 1600 images (700 healthy, 700 melanoma). Original testing set of 105 images (52 healthy, 52 melanoma) was expanded to 400 images (300 healthy, 300 melanoma). Implemented using the Albumentations library.

conclusions: The proposed workflow using transfer learning-based deep learning models achieved greater than 95% accuracy for nail melanoma classification, with ResNet152V2 performing best (96% accuracy after data augmentation).

Post-quantization techniques (TFLite compression) effectively reduced the memory footprint of the deep learning models, making them suitable for deployment on memory-constrained mobile and edge devices without significant loss in performance.

Data augmentation significantly improved the accuracy of all tested models, with ResNet152V2 maintaining the highest performance among the seven CNN architectures evaluated (VGG19, ResNet101, ResNet152V2, Xception, InceptionV3, MobileNet, and MobileNetv2).

The study demonstrates that the proposed workflow is robust and can be readily applied for the timely and accurate early detection of nail melanoma, potentially extending to other types of skin cancer.
Balancing model parameters appropriately allows for improved detection accuracy in lightweight disease detection models while maintaining data throughput, addressing the limitations of edge devices.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, custom-built dataset (Nail-Melanoma-350) collected from a single institution (Dow International Medical College, Karachi, Pakistan), limiting external validity and generalizability.
The dataset is heavily augmented (expanded from 350 to 2000 images) to improve performance, which may lead to optimistic performance estimates that do not reflect real-world data scarcity.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported as descriptive metrics.

The paper does not report an independent external validation set; the testing split is derived from the same source as the training data, raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting to the specific collection site.

The primary goal of the study is model compression (quantization) for edge deployment rather than maximizing diagnostic accuracy, so the reported accuracies (<96%) are secondary to memory footprint reduction.
The dataset size (350 original images) is very small for deep learning, making the results potentially unstable and sensitive to the specific train/test split.

The paper claims >95% accuracy in the abstract/conclusions but the detailed results table shows lower accuracy for quantized models (e.g., ResNet152V2 at 91% accuracy post-quantization), indicating a discrepancy or that the >95% figure refers to pre-quantization or augmented performance not fully detailed in the final comparison table.
No comparison is made against human dermatologists or other state-of-the-art public benchmarks for nail melanoma, limiting the assessment of clinical relevance.
